critic_raw:
This transformer vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment contains several important methodological flaws.

1. EXPLICIT ACKNOWLEDGMENT OF TEMPORAL LEAKAGE WITH INCORRECT JUSTIFICATION. The design states: 'While the introduction of future data might occur, the benefit of consistent class distribution is prioritized to achieve more effective model training.' This is a direct admission that temporal leakage exists, followed by an invalid justification. 'Consistent class distribution' can be achieved with a temporal split by stratifying on the outcome within each temporal window. Accepting leakage to obtain class balance is not a methodological tradeoff — it is an invalid design that will produce optimistic performance estimates that cannot be deployed against future patients.

2. RETRAINED ON TRAINING+VALIDATION AFTER SELECTION INTRODUCES DISTRIBUTIONAL SHIFT. The design specifies: 'After selection, both models are retrained on the combined training + validation data using the selected hyperparameters.' This is problematic for two reasons. First, the hyperparameters were tuned to training-only gradients; retraining on a larger combined dataset may require different learning rate schedules and regularization strengths, meaning the selected hyperparameters may no longer be optimal. Second, the early stopping patience of 5 epochs was calibrated to training-set overfitting signals; without a separate held-out validation set during final training, there is no overfitting signal and the model will train to convergence on different dynamics.

3. CLINICAL BERT WITH FROZEN WEIGHTS CREATES AN UNEXPLORED FEATURE QUALITY FLOOR. The design uses Clinical BERT with frozen weights for discharge note embedding. Frozen pre-trained representations will not be adapted to this hospital's documentation style, abbreviation conventions, or note structure. The embedded representations are fixed at ImageNet-era pretraining quality. The claim that 'any performance difference is attributable to model architecture' is false when the transformer model is using contextual embeddings while the baseline uses the same frozen BERT representation — meaning both models get identical input features. There is no architecture comparison being made; there is a downstream head comparison.

4. F1-SCORE AS PRIMARY METRIC WITH MACRO/WEIGHTED AMBIGUITY. The design states 'Primary metric: F1-score (macro-averaged or weighted)' — the 'or' is operationally critical and unresolved. Macro-F1 and weighted-F1 can tell opposite stories in class-imbalanced settings: a model that improves precision on the majority class but hurts minority-class recall can show macro-F1 decrease and weighted-F1 increase simultaneously. Model selection on the validation set cannot proceed if the primary metric is ambiguous.

5. ZIP CODE-DERIVED SES PROXY MAY INTRODUCE ILLEGAL DISCRIMINATION WITHOUT DETECTION. The design includes 'zip code-derived SES proxy' as an adjusting covariate. Zip code correlates with race/ethnicity in the United States at rates that can function as a race proxy even when race is not an explicit feature. Including it for 'both models equally' does not eliminate this concern — both models may learn a racially discriminatory decision boundary that would be invisible in aggregate metrics but detectable only in stratified analysis. The design mentions subgroup analysis but does not pre-specify a threshold for disparity that would constitute a finding.
all_issues_raised: Design explicitly acknowledges temporal leakage from future data but incorrectly justifies it on class balance grounds; class balance is achievable without temporal leakage
Retraining on combined train+validation set with hyperparameters tuned on train-only dynamics may produce suboptimal models due to changed learning dynamics
Clinical BERT with frozen weights means both transformer and baseline receive identical feature representations; comparison tests head architecture, not transformer capacity
Primary metric ambiguity between macro-F1 and weighted-F1 allows post-hoc selection of whichever metric favors the hypothesis
Zip code-derived SES proxy may function as a race proxy, introducing illegal discrimination that is invisible in aggregate metrics
Design explicitly acknowledges temporal leakage from random split and accepts it, amplifying the transformer's capacity advantage
ClinicalBERT fine-tuned on discharge summaries learns to decode clinician prognosis rather than independent predictive signal
Patient-level independence not enforced; same patient may appear in training and test in a single-system EHR
Length of stay interaction feature uses post-discharge information not available at the intended prediction timepoint
Primary evaluation metric not specified; AUROC does not reflect operational cost asymmetry of false negatives vs. false positives
Stratified random split acknowledged to introduce future data contamination, making the evaluation non-prospective despite the clinical deployment framing
Retraining on combined train+validation data after hyperparameter selection does not produce an unbiased test estimate as claimed
Transformer encoder applied to a single concatenated vector has no sequence to attend over, reducing self-attention to a degenerate linear projection
Primary metric stated as macro-averaged or weighted F1 without resolving which is used, leaving the success criterion undefined
